message.content: Unit tests are small, automated pieces of code that verify the behavior of individual functions or components in a larger program, ensuring they produce the expected outputs for given inputs. They act like checkpoints, running quickly and repeatedly to catch bugs early in development. By isolating specific units of code, such as a single method or class, unit tests make it easier to debug issues since failures point directly to the problematic area. They're useful because they provide a safety net during refactoring—developers can change code confidently knowing tests will flag any breakage. Additionally, unit tests serve as living documentation, clearly illustrating how the code is intended to work. High test coverage boosts code quality and speeds up feature delivery by reducing time spent on manual testing. Overall, incorporating unit tests promotes reliable, maintainable software that evolves without introducing regressions.
